title: About
text: This blog covers our UBC Master of Data Science capstone project with the Trilemma Foundation. We built tools to turn StatsBomb event data into tactical insights that scouts, analysts, and fans can use without writing SQL.

title: About
text: This blog covers our UBC Master of Data Science capstone project with the Trilemma Foundation. We built tools to turn StatsBomb event data into tactical insights that scouts, analysts, and fans can use without writing SQL.

title: About
text:
Who This Blog Is For
We wrote this for people who:

Want to know how analytics work in modern football
Have heard terms like clustering or machine learning but want a plain explanation
Care about player roles and club vs national consistency
Follow how clubs and national teams use data

You do not need a data science background to read it.

title: About
text:
About the Project
This was our DSCI 591 Capstone at UBC, in partnership with the Trilemma Foundation. Our team (Ngoc Minh Quan Hoang, Teem Kwong, Rabindranath Duran Pons, and Li Pu) built an automated analytics platform on StatsBomb open data across 16 competitions and over 12 million on-ball events.
The platform includes player archetype labels, club-vs-national consistency scores, a Looker Studio dashboard, and a plain-English chatbot. Everything runs from a single docker compose up.

title: About
text:
About Me
I’m a Master of Data Science student who likes sports. This blog is a shorter, more readable version of our capstone work.
Thanks for reading.

title: Same Player, Different Game
text:
This post focuses on what we found. For the full pipeline, data warehouse design, clustering methodology, and consistency scoring, see our technical companion post.
Every football fan has felt it: a player who looks unstoppable every weekend for their club, clinical and dominant, always exactly where they need to be. Then the international break arrives, they pull on the national shirt, and something just feels off, not because of injury or bad luck, but because they seem like a different player altogether. Is it the system, the teammates, or are some players genuinely built for one context more than the other?
We spent 2 months trying to answer that question with data. We are four UBC Master of Data Science students who happen to be football fans with strong opinions about pressing systems, squad balance, and why your favourite striker goes quiet at tournaments. For our capstone project with the Trilemma Foundation, we turned 12 million raw football events into a platform that can actually measure the club-vs-country gap, player by player, feature by feature.
With the World Cup here, the timing felt right to share what we found.

title: Delivering Elite European Football Analytics
text:
A national team coach picking a squad for the World Cup faces a common question: does a player who looks great at club level actually play the same way for their country? Scouts face a related one. How do you find pressing forwards in La Liga without writing SQL for every search?
These are the kinds of questions football analysts work on every day. With the World Cup on the calendar, the club-vs-national gap matters even more. A striker who dominates in the league might not fit how a national team actually sets up in a tournament.
For our UBC Master of Data Science capstone with the Trilemma Foundation, we tried to answer these questions by building an automated analytics platform on top of StatsBomb open data.



Official FIFA World Cup 2026 logo.


Each match produces thousands of tracked events like passes, carries, pressures, and shots. The data is there. The harder part is turning roughly 12 million raw events into something scouts, coaches, and analysts can actually use. That was the goal of our project.
A note on the data: our club league analysis focuses on the 2015/2016 season for the top five European leagues (La Liga, Premier League, Bundesliga, Serie A, and Ligue 1), where StatsBomb open data has full seasonal coverage. The wider dataset also includes international tournaments such as the FIFA World Cup, UEFA Euro, Copa América, and AFCON, which we use for the club-vs-national consistency work.
In this post we explain what we built, how the models work, and what we found about player roles and consistency across club and country. You do not need a SQL or machine learning background to follow along.

title: Delivering Elite European Football Analytics
text:
The Problem
StatsBomb open data is one of the most detailed public football datasets available (StatsBomb 2024). It tracks on-ball actions at sub-second level across 16 competitions in Europe, South America, North America, and international tournaments. That includes shots, passes, carries, pressures, dribbles, and more.
The issue is that the data is hard to work with at scale. Records are nested JSON, competitions are split across many files, and pulling useful player metrics means writing complex SQL. Analysts without coding skills were basically locked out.
Trilemma needed a product that could answer tactical questions without a custom query every time. Things like which players fit a pressing role, or who plays differently for club vs country. We turned that into three research questions:

RQ1 (Player archetypes): What tactical roles exist across European football, and how do they differ by position and competition?
RQ2 (Cross-context consistency): Do players keep similar profiles for club and country, and who specializes in one setting?
RQ3 (Natural language access): Can a non-technical analyst query the dataset in plain English and get accurate answers quickly?

title: Delivering Elite European Football Analytics
text:
The Data
All data comes from the StatsBomb open dataset through the statsbombpy Python library. We ingested 16 competitions, including the top five European leagues, the FIFA World Cup, UEFA Euro, Copa América, and AFCON. For club football, the main analysis season is 2015/2016, when StatsBomb provides complete match coverage across La Liga, the Premier League, Bundesliga, Serie A, and Ligue 1.
Three tables sit at the core:



Table
What it contains
Scale




Matches
One row per match
3,464 matches


Events
One row per on-ball action
~12.2 million rows


Lineups
One row per player appearance
165,820 rows



The events table drives most of the analysis. Passes are the most common action (~3.4M), followed by ball receipts (~3.2M), carries (~2.6M), and pressures (~1.1M). There are 88,023 shots with expected goals (xG) values, covering 10,808 players across 308 teams.
A few constraints shaped how we built the models:

270-minute minimum. We only score players with at least 270 minutes in a season, about three full matches. Below that, one good game can skew per-90 stats.
Men’s senior competitions only. Open data does not cover everything. La Liga has full seasonal coverage, but some Champions League history is scattered.
Data quality. Missing substitution times and positions meant we had to clean the data carefully before modelling.

title: Delivering Elite European Football Analytics
text:
How the Analytics Work
We focused on how players play, not just goals scored. Two models use the same 13 per-90 StatsBomb features for attack, ball progression, defending, and efficiency:

PCA + K-Means clustering for tactical archetypes
Within-context z-score scoring for club vs national consistency

Both run on the same automated pipeline:



Automated ELT pipeline: StatsBomb API through BigQuery and dbt to analytics marts, ML outputs, and end-user interfaces.


StatsBomb data goes into Google Cloud Storage, then BigQuery. dbt handles the transforms (staging, intermediate, marts). Python scripts add the ML outputs. A Django app serves a Looker Studio dashboard and a Groq chatbot. Dagster runs weekly full refreshes and hourly updates when new files land.

Player Archetype Clustering
We cluster player-seasons after applying the 270-minute minimum (n = 5,469 outfield players, 5,759 including goalkeepers). Goalkeepers are left out of K-Means and get a fixed label because their event profiles look nothing like outfield players.
The 13 per-90 metrics cover the full pitch:

Attacking: xG per 90, shots per 90, xG per shot
Progression: passes, progressive passes, pass completion %, carries, dribbles
Defending: pressures, interceptions, blocks, clearances, duels

We median-impute missing values, clip outliers at the 99th percentile, and standardize features. PCA keeps components that explain 80% of variance. K-Means with k = 5 runs on that reduced space and gives five outfield archetypes.

Choosing k and validating clusters
We did not pick k = 5 by default. The full selection workflow is in Notebook 05 and the exploratory player clustering notebook; production code lives in src/ml/cluster.py.
PCA variance threshold. We retain the smallest number of principal components that explain at least 80% of total variance (typically six with the final 13-feature set). PC1 loads on overall involvement volume; PC2 contrasts defensive actions against attacking output. The scree plot below shows where extra components add less marginal information.



Cumulative explained variance by PCA component. The 80% threshold typically requires six components on the final feature set.


Elbow method (inertia). We tested k = 2–10 on the PCA-reduced space. Inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) drops sharply from k = 2 through k = 4, with diminishing returns after k = 5 — the classic elbow pattern. That suggested five to six tactically distinct groups rather than a coarse high- vs low-activity split.
Silhouette score. Silhouette measures how well each player sits in its assigned cluster versus the nearest alternative (higher is better). It peaked at k = 2 (≈ 0.34), which is common when per-90 profiles form a continuum rather than hard natural boundaries — a two-cluster solution would mostly separate high- vs low-involvement players, not coach-facing roles. Among k = 3–7, k = 5 offered the best trade-off between separation (silhouette ≈ 0.28), interpretable archetype profiles aligned with football analytics practice, and stable cluster sizes. We did not use the Davies–Bouldin index in the final pipeline; inertia, silhouette, and manual profile review drove the choice instead.



Elbow curve (inertia) and silhouette scores for k = 2–10 on PCA-reduced outfield player-seasons.


Reproducibility and operational stability. Production clustering fixes random_state = 42 and n_init = 10 so assignments are deterministic on the same input data. We did not run bootstrap resampling or adjusted Rand indices in the shipped pipeline; when the underlying player pool grows, archetype labels in seeds/cluster_labels.csv are reviewed manually to stay interpretable. Unit tests in tests/test_cluster.py guard preprocessing (median imputation, 99th-percentile clipping, standardization) and output shape.
External validity. Chi-square tests on position × archetype cross-tabs (Notebook 07) provide a downstream check: center backs concentrate in Defensive Anchor and Low Activity, while forwards skew toward Pressing Forward and Creative Winger. The clusters align with football intuition rather than arbitrary splits.



Table 1: Player archetype distribution across all player-seasons.










Archetype
Player-seasons
Tactical profile




Low Activity
1,963
Low volume across all metrics. Squad players and limited roles


Defensive Anchor
1,470
High clearances, interceptions, duels. Low attacking output


Creative Playmaker
807
High pass completion, progressive passes, carries. Attacking midfielders


Pressing Forward
766
High pressures, shots, and xG. High-energy forwards and pressing wingers


Creative Winger
463
High dribbles, carries, shots. Direct wide attackers


Goalkeeper
290
Assigned by position. Excluded from K-Means






Archetypes describe how a player is used in a given season, not a fixed career label. Lionel Messi is mostly Creative Winger, but shows up as Pressing Forward in Copa América 2024. That fits Argentina’s more compact setup in that tournament.



Looker cluster explorer filtered to Lionel Messi. Most seasons are Creative Winger, with Pressing Forward appearing in Copa América 2024.





Club vs National Consistency Scoring
We looked at 489 dual-context players with at least 270 minutes in both club and national football. Each of the 13 features is standardized within context. Club z-scores compare a player to other club players only. National z-scores compare to other national-team players only.
From there we compute:

Performance scores: how strong a player is vs peers in each setting
Consistency score: how similar a player’s profile is across contexts (1 minus the mean absolute z-gap)
Four performance quadrants: based on median splits on club and national performance




Table 2: Quadrant assignment logic.





Club vs median
National vs median
Quadrant




≥ median
≥ median
Elite


≥ median
< median
Club Specialist


< median
≥ median
International Specialist


< median
< median
Underperformer






Progressive passing into the attacking third was the biggest gap between club-focused and national-focused specialists. An Elite player beats cohort medians in both settings. But a stable profile is not the same as a strong one. Many players look similar across contexts and still rank below average in both.

Validating consistency scores
The consistency model is simpler than clustering, but we still checked it end to end:

Within-context standardization ensures club z-scores compare only to other club players (and national z-scores to national-team peers), so league or tournament level differences do not dominate the gap metric.
Eligibility filter: only players with ≥ 270 minutes in both contexts enter the cohort (n = 489), matching the clustering minimum and stabilizing per-90 rates.
Formula checks: the weighted performance score and consistency formula (1 − mean|z_gap|) are unit-tested in tests/test_consistency.py and walked through in Notebook 03.
Pipeline alignment: end-to-end validation confirmed matching row counts across dbt marts and ML outputs (5,759 clustered player-seasons; 489 dual-context players scored). See the consistency methodology appendix and docs/consistency.md for full definitions.
Face validity: outputs were sanity-checked against known dual-context players (e.g., Serge Gnabry as International Specialist in the dashboard explorer).

title: Same Player, Different Game
text:
What We Found

Five Roles That Cover Almost Every Outfield Player
We ran 5,469 outfield player-seasons through a clustering model built on 13 per-90 metrics covering attack, ball progression, and defending. Five tactical profiles emerge naturally from the data with no hand-labelling and no pre-set categories:



Table 1: Player archetype distribution across all player-seasons.










Archetype
Player-seasons
What the data says




Low Activity
1,963
Low volume across all metrics. Squad players and rotational roles


Defensive Anchor
1,470
High clearances, interceptions, duels. Low attacking output


Creative Playmaker
807
High pass completion, progressive passes, carries. Attacking midfielders


Pressing Forward
766
High pressures, shots, and xG. High-energy forwards and pressing wingers


Creative Winger
463
High dribbles, carries, shots. Direct wide attackers






What is striking is how well these map onto roles any football fan would recognise, and where they surprise you is even more interesting.


Messi the Pressing Forward (No, Really)
During the 2015/2016 La Liga season, Lionel Messi profiled as a Creative Winger, dribbling, carrying, cutting inside, and operating in the half-spaces, the profile most people associate with him. At Copa América 2024, the data classified him as a Pressing Forward. Argentina’s tournament setup asked something completely different from him: he pressed higher, contributed more defensively, and carried less. The event data picks it up automatically. This is not a decline story or an injury story, but a tactical adaptation story that shows up clearly in the numbers:



Looker cluster explorer filtered to Lionel Messi. Most seasons are Creative Winger, with Pressing Forward appearing in Copa América 2024.




Position Shapes Your Role More Than League Does
The bigger pattern across all 5,469 player-seasons is that where you play on the pitch predicts your archetype more than which league you play in. Center backs overwhelmingly land in Defensive Anchor or Low Activity, while forwards and attacking midfielders cluster into Pressing Forward and Creative Winger, which is largely what we would expect. The more interesting finding is how central midfielders spread across three archetypes (Creative Playmaker, Pressing Forward, and Low Activity), reflecting the genuine tactical range of box-to-box roles. A Bundesliga box-to-box midfielder and a Serie A deep-lying playmaker look completely different in the data, even though both appear as “central midfield” on a lineup sheet.
League effects are real but secondary:



Table 2: Outfield cluster counts by competition. Goalkeepers excluded.











Competition
Largest archetype
Second largest
Notable pattern




La Liga
Defensive Anchor (304)
Low Activity (167)
Highest volume, anchors dominate


Serie A
Low Activity (240)
Defensive Anchor (138)
Most conservative profile among the top five


Ligue 1
Defensive Anchor (175)
Low Activity (148)
Similar anchor vs passive mix


Premier League
Defensive Anchor (169)
Low Activity (163)
Near-even top two


Bundesliga
Defensive Anchor (145)
Low Activity (121)
Most Pressing Forwards (83)






Bundesliga clubs press harder on average and Serie A squads sit deeper on average, so if you are scouting across leagues, an archetype comparison only makes sense once you control for position first.


The Club-vs-Country Gap Is Tactical, Not About Quality
Of 489 players with meaningful minutes in both club and national football, about one in three showed a genuinely different profile in each context. Here is how the cohort breaks down:



Table 3: How 489 players with meaningful club and national team minutes split across four club vs country groups. Elite players rank above average in both settings. Club Specialists are strong for their club but weaker for their country. International Specialists are the opposite. Underperformers rank below average in both.





Quadrant
Players
Share




Elite
157
32.1%


Underperformer
156
31.9%


Club Specialist
88
18.0%


International Specialist
88
18.0%






Elite players beat the peer median in both club and national settings. Club Specialists are strong against league peers but dip below their national-team cohort, while International Specialists are the reverse: players who elevate at tournament level. Underperformers sit below median in both contexts. The biggest difference between Club Specialists and International Specialists was not in goals or xG, but in progressive passing into the attacking third and carries into the final third per 90. The club-vs-country gap is mostly a system and role story, not a talent story.


Gnabry’s Hidden International Pedigree
Serge Gnabry is the clearest example from our cohort. When we filter the Consistency Explorer to Germany, his position on the scatter plot tells the story immediately: looking only at his Bundesliga club-season data, he looks solid but unremarkable relative to peers, but in Germany’s national-team context he stands out as a textbook International Specialist, elevating well above his national-team cohort. If you were selecting a Germany squad based purely on club form, you might undervalue him, but knowing he is an International Specialist changes the conversation entirely.



Looker Consistency Explorer filtered to Germany. Serge Gnabry appears as an International Specialist, performing stronger relative to national-team peers than to club peers.




Ask It in Plain English
The dashboard handles regular reporting and sharing, but scouts and analysts always have one-off questions that fixed charts cannot cover, like “Who are the pressing forwards in Ligue 1 with above-average xG?” or “Which Spanish players are Elite in both contexts?” For those, we built a plain-English chatbot that queries the warehouse directly. You type your question, BigQuery SQL is generated and executed in the background, and you receive a formatted answer in under ten seconds. Advanced Mode shows the SQL for anyone who wants to verify or extend the query, and a read-only guard blocks anything destructive.



Plain-English chatbot interface with Advanced Mode enabled. A user asks a natural language question about pressing forwards in Ligue 1, the system generates and executes BigQuery SQL against the warehouse, and returns a formatted table of results alongside the underlying query for verification.


You can try everything yourself at http://34.11.235.252/. The full product walkthrough is in our demo video.

title: Delivering Elite European Football Analytics
text:
The Product
We shipped a Django web app with two interfaces:

Looker Studio dashboard: pre-computed results (archetype distributions, consistency quadrants, player rankings) with filters for position, competition, and archetype. Useful for regular reporting and sharing with non-technical staff.
Plain-English chatbot: handles ad-hoc questions that fixed dashboard charts do not cover, with SQL shown for audit.

You can try the live app yourself at http://34.11.235.252/. It includes the dashboard and the chatbot, with suggested questions like “What cluster does Messi belong to?” or “Who are the Elite players in La Liga?” For a walkthrough of how it works, watch our demo video.
We built both because analysts need broad visual exploration and specific one-off queries. Notebooks alone were not practical for non-technical users. A dashboard alone could not replace flexible querying without Looker edit access.
Strengths: Dagster refreshes all data weekly with no manual steps. The full product runs from a single docker compose up and is documented for handover.
Limitations: the chatbot only handles single-step queries. Multi-step questions (like tracking a player’s archetype across seasons before comparing national peers) are not supported yet. Both interfaces cover men’s competitions only. Dashboard charts cannot be changed without Looker Studio access.

title: Same Player, Different Game
text:
Why This Matters
None of these outputs answer whether a player is worth signing or whether a squad will win a tournament. Archetypes describe how a player is deployed, not their value, and consistency scores measure how similar a profile is across contexts. A stable Underperformer is still an underperformer. But in the right hands, these outputs change the conversation.
For national team staff, consistency scores show which players from your league-season shortlist are likely to translate to a tournament system, and which are club specialists who may struggle with the tactical shift. Gnabry’s International Specialist label is a conversation starter rather than a verdict, but it is a data-grounded one.
For scouts, archetype filters let you find pressing forwards in Ligue 1 with above-average xG without writing SQL for every search. The chatbot handles the querying, and you handle the judgment.
For fans, the next time a player looks completely different at a World Cup, there is a real chance the data backs you up. Systems shape profiles, and Messi pressing hard at Copa América 2024 is not a decline but Argentina’s tournament setup asking something specific from him, and him delivering it.

title: Same Player, Different Game
text:
Conclusion
After 2 months, we built a working platform that can measure the club vs country gap player by player, without writing SQL. But the honest read is that our answers are only as good as the data behind them, and that data is skewed.

The Questions, Answered

Scouts ask: how do I find players who fit a specific tactical role without watching every match in La Liga? Our answer: five tactical archetypes emerge from per-90 event data, and the dashboard plus plain-English chatbot let you filter by role, league, and position. You can search for pressing forwards in Ligue 1 with above-average xG without watching every match. Position predicts role more than league does, so always filter by position before comparing across competitions.
National team coaches ask: which of my in-form players will actually translate to a tournament system? Our answer: consistency scores and club vs country groups flag who is likely to carry club form into a national setup. About one in three players with meaningful club and national team minutes play a genuinely different role in each setting. Gnabry is a clear example: solid at club level, but an International Specialist for Germany. Club Specialists in our data may struggle when the tactical system changes.
Analysts ask: is this player genuinely consistent across contexts, or are we assuming so because they are a big name? Our answer: the data says reputation is not enough. About one in three players with club and national team minutes show a different profile in each setting, and the biggest gaps are in progressive passing and carries, not goals or xG. Messi at Copa América 2024 is the headline example: Creative Winger for most club seasons, Pressing Forward for Argentina.



A Note on What We Cannot Claim
None of this is definitive. StatsBomb open data over-represents the 2015/2016 La Liga season, club archetypes are anchored to that single season, and international tournament minutes are uneven across players and competitions. A player labelled Club Specialist or Pressing Forward in our platform might look different with fuller coverage of the Premier League, Bundesliga, or more recent seasons. Use these outputs to start a conversation, not to close one.
The platform refreshes weekly, the full product deploys with a single docker compose up, and yes, Copa América 2024 Messi is a Pressing Forward in our data. Whether that holds with better-balanced data is a question for the next version of the pipeline.


Key Takeaways

One in three players with club and national team minutes specialises in one setting. The biggest gaps are in progressive passing and carries, not goals or xG.
Gnabry is a textbook International Specialist. Undervalued if you only watch his club seasons.
Messi is classified as Creative Winger for most club seasons but Pressing Forward at Copa América 2024. Argentina’s compact system asked something different from him.
Position drives role assignment more than league. Context matters before you compare leagues.
A plain-English chatbot lets any analyst query 12 million events without SQL.
Data is skewed toward the 2015/2016 La Liga season. Treat archetype labels and consistency scores as provisional, not ground truth.

title: Same Player, Different Game
text:
Quick Reference: Key Terms
Archetype: A tactical role label (e.g., Pressing Forward, Creative Playmaker) based on a player’s per-90 event profile in a given season.
Per-90 metrics: Stats scaled to a full 90-minute game so players with different minutes can be compared fairly.
Consistency score: How similar a player’s tactical profile is for club vs country. A score of 1 means identical profiles. A score of 0 means completely different.
xG (Expected Goals): A shot quality estimate based on distance, angle, and defensive pressure.
Quadrant: One of four groups (Elite, Club Specialist, International Specialist, Underperformer) based on whether a player beats their peer median in each context.
Data warehouse: A database optimised for analytical queries. It is what lets you ask “who are the top pressing forwards in La Liga?” and get an answer in a second instead of an hour.

title: Football Analytics
text:
Same Player, Different Game


A data-driven look at football’s biggest puzzle: why your club’s best player can be a completely different beast for their national team



football

sports analytics

machine learning

capstone









Jun 15, 2026


Ngoc Minh Quan Hoang, Teem Kwong, Rabindranath Duran Pons, Li Pu





No matching items

title: Why Does Messi Play Differently at the World Cup?
text:
The Football Questions Worth Asking
Every summer transfer window, every World Cup squad announcement, the same arguments play out. Is this player good enough for the national team? Why does a striker who scores every weekend go quiet at tournaments? Why does a winger who looks average at club level suddenly become unplayable for their country?
These are not just pub arguments. Scouts ask: how do I find players who fit a specific tactical role, without watching every match in La Liga? National team coaches ask: which of my club-form players will actually translate to a tournament system? Analysts ask: is this player genuinely consistent across contexts, or are we just assuming so?
The data exists to answer all of these. StatsBomb open data (StatsBomb 2024) tracks every on-ball action — passes, carries, pressures, shots, dribbles — at sub-second resolution across 16 competitions: the top five European leagues, the FIFA World Cup, UEFA Euro, Copa América, and AFCON. That is 3,464 matches, 12.2 million events, and 10,808 players.
The problem is that “the data exists” is not the same as “the data is usable.” And that gap is exactly where we spent most of our time.

title: Same Player, Different Game
section: Why You Need More Than a Spreadsheet
text:
Why You Need More Than a Spreadsheet
StatsBomb data at this scale arrives as deeply nested JSON files, one per match, spread across hundreds of files and split across competitions and seasons. Just loading one season of passes for La Liga requires parsing thousands of records, restructuring arrays, and joining multiple tables. For a one-off question, that is annoying. For a scout who needs answers every week, it is a blocker.
This is why we built a data warehouse. Not because it sounds impressive, but because it is the only practical way to turn 12 million raw events into answers you can get in seconds.
Think of it this way. If every touch of the ball in every match is one row in a spreadsheet, you have 12 million rows. Finding the top pressing forwards across five leagues means scanning all of them, calculating rates per 90 minutes, filtering out players with too few appearances, and ranking by position. In a spreadsheet, that takes hours and breaks. In a warehouse with pre-built summaries, it takes a second.
We built the full pipeline on Google Cloud. Raw data lands in Google Cloud Storage, moves into BigQuery (Google’s cloud data warehouse), and gets transformed by dbt into clean, analysis-ready tables. Those tables feed two machine learning models, a Looker Studio dashboard, and a plain-English chatbot, all refreshed weekly by Dagster with no manual steps.
The warehouse is the invisible foundation. Without it, every question requires a programmer and a few hours. With it, any analyst can ask “who are the elite pressing forwards in Ligue 1?” and get an answer in under ten seconds.



Automated ELT pipeline: StatsBomb API through BigQuery and dbt to analytics marts, ML outputs, and end-user interfaces.

title: Why Does Messi Play Differently at the World Cup?
text:
What We Actually Found

Messi’s Two Identities — and Five Roles for Everyone Else
We ran 5,469 outfield player-seasons through an unsupervised clustering model (PCA + K-Means on 13 per-90 metrics covering attack, ball progression, and defending). The algorithm found five distinct tactical profiles that emerge naturally from the data:



Table 1: Player archetype distribution across all player-seasons.










Archetype
Player-seasons
What the data says




Low Activity
1,963
Low volume across all metrics. Squad players and rotational roles


Defensive Anchor
1,470
High clearances, interceptions, duels. Low attacking output


Creative Playmaker
807
High pass completion, progressive passes, carries. Attacking midfielders


Pressing Forward
766
High pressures, shots, and xG. High-energy forwards and pressing wingers


Creative Winger
463
High dribbles, carries, shots. Direct wide attackers






These are not hand-labelled categories. They come entirely from the event data. What is striking is how well they map onto roles any football fan would recognise — and where they surprise you.
Messi across seasons and competitions is a case study in how tactical systems shape profiles. Most of his club seasons land in Creative Winger. But Copa América 2024 is different:



Looker cluster explorer filtered to Lionel Messi. Most seasons are Creative Winger, with Pressing Forward appearing in Copa América 2024.


Argentina’s tournament setup asked him to press higher, contribute more defensively, and carry less. The event profile reflects that — and the model picks it up automatically. This is not a decline story. It is a tactical adaptation story, and the data tells it clearly.


Position Matters More Than League (But League Still Matters)
The bigger pattern across all 5,469 player-seasons: where you play on the pitch shapes your archetype more than which league you play in.
Center backs overwhelmingly end up in Defensive Anchor or Low Activity. Forwards and attacking midfielders cluster into Pressing Forward and Creative Winger. That much is expected. The more interesting finding is how central midfielders spread across three archetypes — Creative Playmaker, Pressing Forward, and Low Activity — which reflects the genuine tactical range of box-to-box roles. A Bundesliga box-to-box midfielder and a Serie A deep-lying playmaker carry very different event profiles, even though both show up as “central midfield” on a lineup sheet.
League effects are real but secondary:



Table 2: Outfield cluster counts by competition. Goalkeepers excluded.











Competition
Largest archetype
Second largest
Notable pattern




La Liga
Defensive Anchor (304)
Low Activity (167)
Highest volume; anchors dominate


Serie A
Low Activity (240)
Defensive Anchor (138)
Most conservative profile among the top five


Ligue 1
Defensive Anchor (175)
Low Activity (148)
Similar anchor vs passive mix


Premier League
Defensive Anchor (169)
Low Activity (163)
Near-even top two


Bundesliga
Defensive Anchor (145)
Low Activity (121)
Most Pressing Forwards (83)






Bundesliga clubs press harder, on average. Serie A squads sit deeper. If you are scouting across leagues, the archetype comparison is only meaningful once you control for position first.


The Club-vs-Country Gap Is Real — and Tactical, Not About Quality
This is the question that matters most around a World Cup. Of 489 players with meaningful minutes in both club and national football, about one in three showed a genuinely different profile in each context. Here is how the cohort breaks down:



Table 3: Observed quadrant counts for dual-context players.





Quadrant
Players
Share




Elite
157
32.1%


Underperformer
156
31.9%


Club Specialist
88
18.0%


International Specialist
88
18.0%






Elite players beat the peer median in both settings. Club Specialists perform above their club cohort but dip below national-team peers. International Specialists are the reverse — players who somehow elevate at tournament level. Underperformers are below median in both.
The biggest difference between Club Specialists and International Specialists was not in goals or xG. It was in progressive passing into the attacking third and carries into the final third per 90. The club-vs-country gap is mostly a system and role story, not a quality story.
Serge Gnabry shows up as a textbook International Specialist: stronger versus national-team peers than versus Bundesliga peers. If you were selecting a Germany squad and only looked at his club form, you might undervalue him.



Looker Consistency Explorer with Germany selected. Serge Gnabry appears as an International Specialist, stronger vs national-team peers than club peers.




Ask It in Plain English
Beyond the models, we built a chatbot so analysts can query the warehouse without writing SQL. Type “Who are the Elite players in La Liga?” or “What cluster does Messi belong to?” and the app generates BigQuery SQL, runs it, and returns a formatted answer in under ten seconds.
Advanced Mode shows the generated SQL — useful for analysts who want to verify or extend the query. A read-only guard blocks anything destructive.



Django chatbot with Advanced Mode enabled, showing a natural language query, generated BigQuery SQL, and formatted results.


You can try it yourself at http://34.11.235.252/. The full product walkthrough is in our demo video.

title: Why Does Messi Play Differently at the World Cup?
text:
How the Models Work
If you want the methodology details, read on. If you are here for the football, feel free to skip to Why This Matters.

The 13 Features Behind Every Archetype
Both models — clustering and consistency scoring — use the same 13 per-90 StatsBomb metrics. Using the same feature set means archetype labels stay aligned with consistency scores across the whole product.

Attacking: xG per 90, shots per 90, xG per shot
Progression: passes, progressive passes, pass completion %, carries, dribbles
Defending: pressures, interceptions, blocks, clearances, duels

We only score players with at least 270 minutes in a season (roughly three full matches). Below that, a single exceptional game can dominate per-90 stats and produce misleading profiles.


Choosing Five Archetypes (Not Two, Not Eight)
We did not pick k = 5 by default. The full selection workflow is in Notebook 05; production code is in src/ml/cluster.py.
PCA first. We reduce the 13 features to the smallest set of principal components explaining 80% of variance — typically six. PC1 captures overall involvement volume; PC2 contrasts defensive vs attacking output.



Cumulative explained variance by PCA component. The 80% threshold typically requires six components on the final feature set.


Then elbow + silhouette to choose k. We tested k = 2 through 10. Inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) drops sharply through k = 4, with diminishing returns after k = 5 — the classic elbow shape. Silhouette score peaked at k = 2 (≈ 0.34), which is typical when per-90 profiles form a continuum rather than hard natural clusters. A k = 2 solution would just separate high- and low-involvement players — not useful for scouting. Among k = 3–7, k = 5 gave the best balance of cluster separation (silhouette ≈ 0.28) and coach-facing interpretability.



Elbow curve (inertia) and silhouette scores for k = 2–10 on PCA-reduced outfield player-seasons.


Reproducibility. Production runs fix random_state = 42 and n_init = 10 so assignments are deterministic. Archetype labels in seeds/cluster_labels.csv are reviewed manually when the player pool grows. Unit tests in tests/test_cluster.py cover preprocessing (median imputation, 99th-percentile clipping, standardization) and output shape. Chi-square tests on position × archetype cross-tabs (Notebook 07) confirm the clusters align with football intuition — center backs in Defensive Anchor, forwards in Pressing Forward — rather than arbitrary splits.


Measuring Club-vs-Country Consistency
For the 489 dual-context players, each feature is standardized within context — club z-scores compare only to other club players; national z-scores compare only to national-team peers. That way, league-level differences (Premier League vs. international tempo) do not inflate the gap.
Performance score (club and national axes):
\[\text{performance score} = \sum_{i=1}^{13} (Z_i \times w_i)\]
where \(w_i\) are PCA-derived feature weights from the clustering pipeline.
Consistency score (profile similarity, not quality):
\[\text{consistency score} = 1 - \frac{1}{13}\sum_{i=1}^{13} \left|Z_{i,\text{club}} - Z_{i,\text{national}}\right|\]
A score of 1 means identical profiles across contexts; 0 means completely different. Quadrants split the cohort at the median of each performance axis.
Validation: the weighted score and consistency formula are unit-tested in tests/test_consistency.py and documented step by step in Notebook 03. End-to-end pipeline checks confirmed matching row counts across dbt marts and ML outputs (5,759 clustered player-seasons; 489 dual-context players scored). See the consistency methodology appendix and docs/consistency.md for full definitions.

title: Same Player, Different Game
section: The Questions Every Fan and Scout Is Already Asking
text:
The Questions Every Fan and Scout Is Already Asking
Every summer transfer window and every World Cup squad announcement brings the same arguments: is this player good enough for the national team, why does a striker who scores every weekend go quiet at tournaments, and why does a winger who looks ordinary at club level suddenly become unplayable internationally?
These are not just pub arguments:

Scouts ask: how do I find players who fit a specific tactical role without watching every match in La Liga?
National team coaches ask: which of my in-form players will actually translate to a tournament system?
Analysts ask: is this player genuinely consistent across contexts, or are we assuming so because they are a big name?

The data exists to answer all of these, but our results come with an important caveat. StatsBomb open data (StatsBomb 2024) tracks every on-ball action including passes, carries, pressures, shots, and dribbles at sub-second resolution across 16 competitions: the top five European leagues, the FIFA World Cup, UEFA Euro, Copa América, and AFCON. That is 3,464 matches, 12.2 million events, and 10,808 players. Coverage is heavily skewed: the 2015/2016 La Liga season alone accounts for over 800 matches and dominates the dataset, so any finding from this analysis may not generalise to other leagues, seasons, or competitions.
One important detail about scope: our club-level archetypes are built on the 2015/2016 season, which is the only season where StatsBomb open data provides complete match coverage across all five top European leagues (La Liga, Premier League, Bundesliga, Serie A, and Ligue 1). La Liga alone accounts for over 800 matches from that season, which is why it dominates the coverage chart below. The club-vs-country consistency analysis draws on a wider window, pairing each player’s 2015/2016 club profile against their international appearances across all tournaments in the dataset. Treat every number in this post as provisional. The skew toward La Liga and a single club season means our archetype labels and consistency scores could be wrong for players or leagues with thinner coverage.



StatsBomb open data coverage overview: 3,429 matches across 16 competitions from 2003 to 2025. The 2015/2016 La Liga season is by far the largest single-season contribution, which is why it anchors the club archetype analysis.


The problem is that “the data exists” is not the same as “the data is usable,” and that gap is exactly where we spent most of our time. We cover the engineering side in our technical companion post. Here is what we actually found.

title: Same Player, Different Game
section: Under the Hood: How the Models Work
text:
Under the Hood: How the Models Work
The technical details for the curious. You can skip this section entirely and still get everything from the findings above.

The 13 Features Behind Every Archetype
Both models (clustering and consistency scoring) run on the same 13 per-90 StatsBomb metrics, so archetype labels and consistency scores stay aligned across the whole product:

Attacking: xG per 90, shots per 90, xG per shot
Progression: passes, progressive passes, pass completion %, carries, dribbles
Defending: pressures, interceptions, blocks, clearances, duels

We only score players with at least 270 minutes in a season (roughly three full matches). Below that, a single exceptional game can dominate per-90 stats and produce misleading profiles.


Choosing Five Archetypes (Not Two, Not Eight)
We did not pick k = 5 by default. The full selection workflow is in Notebook 05 and production code lives in src/ml/cluster.py.
PCA first. We reduce the 13 features to the smallest set of principal components explaining at least 80% of variance, which is typically six. PC1 captures overall involvement volume. PC2 contrasts defensive vs attacking output.



Cumulative explained variance by PCA component. The 80% threshold typically requires six components on the final feature set.


Elbow and silhouette to choose k. We tested k = 2 through 10. Inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) drops sharply through k = 4, with diminishing returns after k = 5, which is the classic elbow shape. Silhouette score peaked at k = 2 (≈ 0.34), which is typical when per-90 profiles form a continuum rather than hard natural clusters. A k = 2 solution would just separate high- and low-involvement players, which is not useful. Among k = 3 through 7, k = 5 gave the best balance of cluster separation (silhouette ≈ 0.28) and tactically meaningful archetypes.



Elbow curve (inertia) and silhouette scores for k = 2–10 on PCA-reduced outfield player-seasons.


Reproducibility. Production runs fix random_state = 42 and n_init = 10 so assignments are deterministic. Archetype labels in seeds/cluster_labels.csv are reviewed manually when the player pool grows. Unit tests in tests/test_cluster.py cover preprocessing and output shape. Chi-square tests on position × archetype cross-tabs (Notebook 07) confirm the clusters align with football intuition rather than arbitrary splits.


Measuring Club-vs-Country Consistency
For the 489 dual-context players, each feature is standardised within context. Club z-scores compare only to other club players. National z-scores compare only to national-team peers. That way, league-level differences in tempo or style do not inflate the gap.
Performance score (club and national axes):
\[\text{performance score} = \sum_{i=1}^{13} (Z_i \times w_i)\]
where \(w_i\) are PCA-derived feature weights from the clustering pipeline.
Consistency score (profile similarity, not quality level):
\[\text{consistency score} = 1 - \frac{1}{13}\sum_{i=1}^{13} \left|Z_{i,\text{club}} - Z_{i,\text{national}}\right|\]
A score of 1 means identical profiles across contexts. A score of 0 means completely different. Quadrants split the cohort at the median of each performance axis.
The weighted score and consistency formula are unit-tested in tests/test_consistency.py and documented in Notebook 03. End-to-end pipeline checks confirmed matching row counts across dbt marts and ML outputs (5,759 clustered player-seasons and 489 dual-context players scored). Full definitions are in the consistency methodology appendix and docs/consistency.md.
